Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after my extracurricular activities or work, but then I'd be too tired or just not in the mood. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when the anxiety about failing got too intense to ignore. I know I should have been more organized, but everything just felt overwhelming and I couldn't bring myself to stick to any schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular at all. I'd go days without opening anything course-related, then suddenly panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything in. Sometimes I'd have shifts or club meetings that took priority, and studying just kept getting pushed back. The only consistent thing was inconsistency, honestly.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—like realizing an assignment was due soon or seeing a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which usually made me feel worse because there was so much. Occasionally a classmate would message me asking about something and that would remind me the LMS existed.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like checking it every morning, but that lasted maybe three days. The mindfulness exercises they recommended helped a bit with my stress, but they didn't translate into better LMS habits. It just never became automatic for me—there was always something else pulling my attention away.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I learn better by watching and doing rather than reading. I could play them in the background sometimes while doing other things, which felt more manageable. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused, which was weirdly comforting.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and the supplementary PDFs. Reading long documents on a screen is exhausting for me, and I don't have money to print everything out. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The practice quizzes I also avoided because failing them just made my anxiety spike even more.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment that was due. If something felt too abstract or like busywork, I just couldn't motivate myself to do it. I needed to feel like I was actually accomplishing something, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on. When you're already drowning in required stuff you haven't done, optional just means 'skip this.' Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, which sometimes was the only thing that could get me moving. Optional stuff just got buried under everything else.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or videos to my phone and look at them on the bus or during breaks at work. I also borrowed a classmate's physical notes a few times because reading handwritten stuff felt easier than staring at screens. Sometimes I'd just talk through concepts with friends from the course instead of formally studying.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded materials or working through problems on paper, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also watch YouTube videos explaining concepts differently than the lectures did. My LMS would look dead, but I was actually trying to understand stuff—just not in ways the system could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never go back to the LMS for it. I'd study from my downloads folder instead, rewatching things or rereading when I had time. It was easier than dealing with the slow internet at my place and the LMS interface that always felt clunky to me.
Q12: Around midterms everything just crashed for me emotionally. I was already behind, my anxiety was through the roof, and I felt like there was no point in even trying anymore. The sadness would hit and I'd just lie in bed scrolling my phone instead of studying. Work was also demanding more hours around that time, which made everything worse.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind, I just couldn't bring myself to log in. Seeing all the incomplete tasks and missed deadlines made my stress unbearable. Sometimes I'd open the LMS, feel the anxiety wash over me, and immediately close it. It became this vicious cycle I couldn't break.
Q14: So many times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, about failing, about everything. I'd technically 'watch' something but retain nothing. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, even though I knew I wasn't actually learning.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important, honestly. The bigger and more important something felt, the more terrifying it was to start. I knew the assignments mattered, but the fear of doing them badly paralyzed me. It's like my brain would rather avoid the pain of trying and failing than actually attempt something.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could force me into action. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then frantically try to throw something together. The LMS would show me being inactive for weeks, then suddenly logging in multiple times in one day. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could function.
Q17: Feedback honestly made things worse sometimes. Getting a bad grade would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for days. Good feedback was rare for me, and when I did get constructive comments, I often felt too demoralized to actually apply them. I know that's counterproductive, but that's where my head was at.
Q18: The course structure felt like it assumed you were keeping up from day one. Once I fell behind, it was like trying to jump onto a moving train. The way modules built on each other meant missing early stuff made later stuff incomprehensible. I needed more flexibility, more chances to catch up, but the structure just kept moving forward without me.
Q19: The rigid deadlines made engagement really hard for someone in my situation. Having sixteen online courses to juggle with limited resources and a job was brutal. What would have helped is more scaffolded support, maybe check-ins or smaller milestones. The mindfulness intervention they offered was nice but didn't address the structural problems making me disengage.
Q20: I think that's someone who's trying but struggling, maybe like me on my better days. You log in with good intentions, watch some videos, click around trying to understand, but when it comes to actually producing work, the anxiety or confusion stops you. High activity doesn't mean high comprehension or confidence—it might just mean high desperation.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Someone could be studying effectively offline—using textbooks, study groups, downloaded materials—and not need the LMS much. Or they might just be naturally quick at grasping things. My low activity definitely didn't mean I wasn't thinking about the course; it just meant I wasn't engaging through the platform specifically.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show what was happening in your head or your life. They miss the emotional barriers, the offline studying, the real reasons behind the numbers. A log might say I watched a video, but it can't say I was crying from stress while it played in the background.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through motions. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand, when the material matters to you somehow. For me, engagement was rare this semester—I was mostly just surviving, not truly engaging. Real engagement feels active, not desperate.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed despite everything, or how hard my circumstances made it to show up. It can't capture the sadness that made getting out of bed feel impossible some days, or the anxiety that made opening the platform feel like facing a monster. My data looks like I didn't care, but the truth is I cared too much and it paralyzed me.
